steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has a clear subject ("The word 'pessimism'") and needs a finite main verb. The time reference is a specific past event (a 1794 letter), so the verb must be simple past tense. Any non-finite form will leave the sentence without a working predicate.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "to appear": An infinitive cannot serve as the main verb of a clause. The sentence has no other main verb, so this creates a fragment. ✗
  • "appeared": Simple past tense, agrees with the singular subject, and correctly places the event in the past. The sentence reads: "The word 'pessimism' first appeared in a 1794 letter" — grammatically complete. ✓
  • "appearing": A present participle cannot function as a main verb without an auxiliary (e.g., "was appearing"). Used alone, it creates a fragment. ✗
  • "having appeared": A perfect participle introduces a participial phrase modifying a noun, not a main clause. This also creates a fragment. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
